Summary:
Relationship structure: causal chain with intermediary mechanisms.

FOXP3 specifies regulatory T cell identity and, by forming a large molecular complex with other transcription factors and enzymatic subunits, is required for the development and dynamic regulation of FOXP3+ Treg cells. In the tumour microenvironment, tumour-infiltrating FOXP3+ Treg cells exert strong immunosuppressive functions that promote cancer immune escape and tumour progression. Mechanistically, FOXP3 stability and Treg function are modulated by epigenetic regulators (e.g., EZH2), PD-1/PD-L1 signaling, chemokine-driven recruitment (e.g., CCL5), and metabolic adaptations (e.g., enhanced glycolysis, lactic acid uptake, glucose competition), resulting in inhibitory receptor expression, immunosuppressive cytokine release, ATP-to-adenosine conversion, and induction of effector T cell senescence. As an outcome, these FOXP3-mediated processes establish an immunosuppressive tumour microenvironment that facilitates tumour growth, metastasis, angiogenesis, and resistance to immunotherapy; destabilizing FOXP3 can reprogram tumour Treg cells toward pro-inflammatory activities and enhance anti-tumour immunity.
References: Qiu, Y., Ke, S., Chen, J., Qin, Z., Zhang, W., Yuan, Y., Meng, D., Zhao, G., Wu, K., Li, B., & Li, D. (2022). FOXP3+ regulatory T cells and the immune escape in solid tumours. Front Immunol, 13, 982986. https://doi.org/10.3389/fimmu.2022.982986
